questions.stem: The treaty was ( ) by all signatory nations within a year.
questions.explanation: ratify a treaty = 条約を批准する。ravage = 破壊する、range = 範囲、rally = 結集する。

questions.stem: His criticism was ( ) but ultimately constructive.
questions.explanation: scathing criticism = 痛烈な批判。scatter = 散らす、scarce = 乏しい、scenic = 景色の良い。

questions.stem: The discovery has ( ) implications for the entire field.
questions.explanation: far-reaching implications = 広範囲に及ぶ意味合い。far-flung = 遠隔の、far-fetched = こじつけの、faraway = 遠くの。

questions.stem: The board ( ) the new policy after extensive discussion.
questions.explanation: endorse a policy = 政策を支持/承認する。engulf = 飲み込む、encroach = 侵害する、enrage = 激怒させる。

questions.stem: The project's success was ( ) on securing additional funding.
questions.explanation: contingent on = 〜次第である/〜を条件とする。contagious = 伝染性、contentious = 議論を呼ぶ、continuous = 連続した。

questions.stem: The lawyer presented a ( ) defense of her client.
questions.explanation: robust defense = 堅固な弁護。ribald = 下品な、rapt = 没頭した、rampant = 蔓延る。

questions.stem: The architectural style is ( ) of late Renaissance influence.
questions.explanation: reminiscent of = 〜を思わせる。resilient = 回復力のある、reluctant = 気が進まない、reverberant = 反響する。

questions.stem: The findings ( ) previous theories about climate patterns.
questions.explanation: contradict theories = 理論と矛盾する。contemplate = 熟考、contaminate = 汚染、contribute = 貢献。

questions.stem: The negotiations reached an ( ).
questions.explanation: reach an impasse = 行き詰まりに達する。impulse = 衝動、imprint = 刻印、import = 輸入。

questions.stem: What ( ) you doing now?
questions.explanation: 主語 you + 進行形 → are + ing。

questions.stem: Mike has more books ( ) Tom does.
questions.explanation: more X than Y = Y より多くの X。比較級 + than。

questions.stem: I'm looking ( ) my keys.
questions.explanation: look for = 〜を探す。look at = 〜を見る、look into = 調査、look up = 調べる/見上げる。

questions.stem: Be careful! The road is ( ).
questions.explanation: 主語 the road を補語で修飾 → 形容詞 slippery (滑りやすい)。

questions.stem: My uncle's birthday is ( ) the third of May.
questions.explanation: 日付の前 → on (the third of May = May 3)。

questions.stem: Hurry up, ( ) we will be late.
questions.explanation: 命令文 + or = 〜しなさい、さもないと…。命令文 + and = 〜しなさい、そうすれば…。

questions.stem: Yumi enjoys ( ) novels in her free time.
questions.explanation: enjoy + 動名詞。enjoy to do は不可。

questions.stem: Don't forget ( ) the lights when you leave.
questions.explanation: forget to do = (これからすることを) 忘れる。forget doing = (した過去を) 忘れる。

questions.stem: There ( ) much milk in the fridge.
questions.explanation: milk = 不可算名詞 → 単数 → isn't。否定+ much = 多くない (不可算)。

questions.stem: Where ( ) you from?
questions.explanation: 主語 you → are。出身を尋ねる Where are you from?

questions.stem: My cat is ( ) the table.
questions.explanation: on = 〜の上に (接触)。

questions.stem: I have ( ) brother and one sister.
questions.explanation: and one sister と並列なので one が自然。a でも文法的だが選択肢では one が並列性を保つ。

questions.stem: What ( ) you do on Sundays? — I read books.
questions.explanation: 主語 you + 一般動詞現在 → do。

questions.stem: Look! The dog ( ) running.
questions.explanation: 3単 the dog + 進行形 → is + ing。

questions.stem: My father is a ( ).
questions.explanation: is a + 単数名詞 → doctor。

questions.stem: I ( ) tennis with my friends yesterday.
questions.explanation: yesterday → 過去形 played。

questions.stem: That ( ) my brother.
questions.explanation: 単数主語 That → is。

questions.stem: How ( ) is this dictionary? — It's $20.
questions.explanation: 値段 → How much。

questions.stem: The new manager has ( ) experience in international business.
questions.explanation: extensive experience = 豊富な経験。extended = 延長された、external = 外部の、extracted = 抽出された。

questions.stem: We need to ( ) the meeting until next week.
questions.explanation: postpone = 延期する。propose = 提案、proceed = 進む、provoke = 挑発。

questions.stem: The company is ( ) for its excellent customer service.
questions.explanation: renowned for = 〜で有名な (名声がある)。

questions.stem: The professor ( ) the importance of independent thinking.
questions.explanation: emphasize the importance = 重要性を強調する。employ = 雇う、enable = 可能にする、enchant = 魅了する。

questions.stem: The doctor recommended that I ( ) more rest.
questions.explanation: recommend that S (should) + 動詞原形 = 仮定法現在。get が原形。

questions.stem: Despite the heavy rain, we ( ) to reach the summit.
questions.explanation: manage to do = なんとか〜することができた。

questions.stem: The athlete ( ) a new world record at the Olympics.
questions.explanation: set a record = 記録を樹立する (set 過去形も set)。sit/sat = 座る。

questions.stem: This issue ( ) immediate attention.
questions.explanation: require attention = 注意を必要とする。resist = 抵抗、retrieve = 回収、resemble = 似ている。

questions.stem: She is ( ) qualified for this position.
questions.explanation: highly qualified = 高度に資格を備えた。heavily = 重く、hardly = ほとんど〜ない、handily = 巧みに/容易に。

questions.stem: My uncle works ( ) a hospital.
questions.explanation: work at + 場所 = 〜で働く。

questions.stem: I'm sure ( ) this exam.
questions.explanation: be sure of + 動名詞 = 〜することを確信している。

questions.stem: This is the man ( ) lives next door.
questions.explanation: 先行詞 the man (人) + 主格関係詞 → who。

questions.stem: The bus ( ) at 8:00 every morning.
questions.explanation: 3単現 (the bus) + 現在の習慣 → leaves。

questions.stem: There are some apples ( ) the table.
questions.explanation: on the table = テーブルの上に。

questions.stem: What time ( ) you usually get up?
questions.explanation: 主語 you + 一般動詞現在 → do。

questions.stem: The committee was forced to ( ) its initial findings.
questions.explanation: revise findings = 知見を修正する。reside = 住む、resort = 頼る、retain = 保持。

questions.stem: The author's ( ) writing style appeals to a wide audience.
questions.explanation: accessible writing style = 読みやすい文体。acceptable = 受容可能な、accountable = 責任を負う、accidental = 偶然の。

questions.stem: The negotiations are at a ( ) stage.
questions.explanation: delicate stage = 微妙な段階。delicious = おいしい、derivative = 派生的、deliberate = 慎重な。

questions.stem: The professor's lecture provided new ( ) into the topic.
questions.explanation: insights into = 〜への洞察。incident = 出来事、inquiry = 問い合わせ、incentive = 動機。

questions.stem: The team's strategy was ( ) brilliant.
questions.explanation: undeniably = 否定し得ないほど。unduly = 過度に、unwittingly = 知らずに、unevenly = 不均一に。

questions.stem: The CEO's ( ) at the meeting was unexpected.
questions.explanation: absence = 不在。abandonment = 放棄、abundance = 豊富、accolade = 賛辞。

questions.stem: The new policy will ( ) take effect next month.
questions.explanation: formally take effect = 正式に発効する。

questions.stem: The judge's decision was ( ) controversial.
questions.explanation: highly controversial = 非常に物議を醸す。

questions.stem: He ( ) the responsibility for the project's failure.
questions.explanation: assume responsibility = 責任を負う。

questions.stem: The library ( ) all weekend last week.
questions.explanation: last week + 受動態過去 → was closed。

questions.stem: It's no use ( ) about the past.
questions.explanation: It's no use + 動名詞 = 〜しても無駄だ。

questions.stem: He always ( ) early to avoid traffic.
questions.explanation: 3単現 + 現在の習慣 → leaves。

questions.stem: I wish I ( ) more time to study.
questions.explanation: I wish + 仮定法過去 (had)。

questions.stem: The book is worth ( ) twice.
questions.explanation: be worth + 動名詞 = 〜する価値がある。

questions.stem: This is the best movie ( ) I have ever seen.
questions.explanation: 先行詞に最上級 → 関係詞 that が好まれる。

questions.stem: He had no choice ( ) accept the offer.
questions.explanation: have no choice but to do = 〜するしかない。

questions.stem: As soon as the rain ( ), we left.
questions.explanation: 主節過去 + 副詞節過去で時制一致。stopped。

questions.stem: The jewelry was hidden ( ) sight.
questions.explanation: out of sight = 視界の外に。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should governments invest in renewable energy more aggressively?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Climate - Cost - Energy security - Jobs

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I strongly support more aggressive government investment in renewable energy. Two arguments support this view: climate change urgency and energy security benefits.

First, the climate crisis demands immediate, large-scale action. The IPCC has warned that limiting warming to 1.5°C requires halving emissions by 2030. Renewable energy directly displaces fossil fuel emissions—the largest single source of greenhouse gases.

Second, renewable energy enhances national energy security. Domestic renewable resources cannot be cut off by hostile nations or disrupted by global price shocks. Recent geopolitical events have demonstrated the vulnerability of fossil-fuel-dependent economies.

While critics emphasize upfront costs, the long-term economics favor renewables: declining costs combined with lower fuel and health expenses make aggressive investment cost-effective over reasonable time horizons. (132 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】Climate + Energy security の 2 points・IPCC データ引用・反論への組込。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Is online learning as effective as traditional classroom learning?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Flexibility - Interaction - Cost - Discipline

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I do not believe online learning is as effective as traditional classroom learning. My reasoning rests on two key factors: limited interaction and required self-discipline.

First, online learning sacrifices the rich interaction that classrooms provide. Spontaneous discussions, immediate clarification, and peer collaboration are difficult to replicate through screens. Studies consistently show that students retain less information in fully online environments.

Second, online learning requires self-discipline that many students lack. Without classroom structure, completion rates for online courses average only 5-15%, compared to over 80% for traditional courses. Younger students particularly struggle with the executive function demands of independent learning.

While online learning offers valuable flexibility, it works best as a supplement to, not a replacement for, traditional classroom education. (128 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】Interaction + Discipline の 2 points・統計引用・supplement 結論。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should social media platforms be more strictly regulated?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Misinformation - Free speech - Mental health - Economy

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I firmly support stricter regulation of social media platforms. Two arguments justify this position: misinformation harm and mental health damage.

First, unregulated platforms have become major vectors for misinformation. False health claims and election conspiracies spread faster than corrections. Studies show false news travels six times faster than accurate news on Twitter, with substantial documented harm to public health and democratic processes.

Second, mental health damage, particularly among adolescents, has reached crisis levels. Internal Facebook research showed Instagram worsens body image issues for one in three teenage girls. Depression and anxiety rates among teens have risen sharply alongside smartphone adoption.

While free speech concerns deserve attention, narrow regulations targeting algorithmic amplification and protecting minors do not threaten legitimate expression. The harm from inaction outweighs theoretical risks. (133 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】Misinformation + Mental health の 2 points・narrow regulation 結論。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should young people travel abroad more often?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Language - Culture - Cost - Safety

Requirements: 80-100 words.
question_data.sample:
I believe young people should travel abroad more often. Two reasons support this view.

First, traveling abroad improves language skills. Real conversations with native speakers cannot be replicated in classrooms. Many young travelers achieve fluency that years of study could not produce.

Second, traveling abroad develops cultural understanding. Experiencing different customs, foods, and perspectives breaks down stereotypes and builds empathy. This understanding becomes increasingly valuable in our globalized world.

While cost concerns are real, budget travel options have made international experiences more affordable than ever. (89 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準2級】Language + Culture の 2 points。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should public transportation be free?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Environment - Cost - Traffic - Equality

Requirements: 80-100 words.
question_data.sample:
I think public transportation should be free. Two reasons support my view.

First, free public transit would significantly help the environment. More riders mean fewer cars on the road, reducing pollution and greenhouse gas emissions. Cities that have tested free transit report substantial decreases in air pollution.

Second, free transit promotes social equality. Lower-income workers spend disproportionate shares of their income on transportation. Free transit gives all citizens equal access to jobs, education, and services regardless of income.

While funding challenges exist, the social and environmental benefits justify the public investment. (94 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準2級】Environment + Equality の 2 points。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Are zoos important for protecting endangered species?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Conservation - Education - Animal welfare - Cost

Requirements: 80-100 words.
question_data.sample:
I believe zoos play an important role in protecting endangered species. Two reasons support this view.

First, zoos directly contribute to conservation. Captive breeding programs have saved species like the California condor and Arabian oryx from extinction. Without zoo intervention, these animals would have been lost forever.

Second, zoos provide vital education. Seeing real animals creates emotional connections that documentaries cannot match. Children who visit zoos develop lifelong interests in wildlife protection.

While concerns about animal welfare are legitimate, modern zoos have transformed into conservation centers focused on species survival. (96 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準2級】Conservation + Education の 2 points・具体例 (California condor 等)。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should young people learn programming at school?

POINTS to consider (use 2): - Future jobs - Logic - Cost - Time

Requirements: 50-60 words.
question_data.sample:
I think young people should learn programming at school. I have two reasons.

First, programming will help students get future jobs. Many companies need workers who can code. Second, programming improves logical thinking. Students learn to break problems into small steps.

For these reasons, programming should be taught at school. (52 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 50-60 word opinion essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級】Future jobs + Logic の 2 points。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Is it better to eat home-cooked meals than restaurant meals?

POINTS to consider (use 2): - Health - Cost - Time - Taste

Requirements: 50-60 words.
question_data.sample:
I think it is better to eat home-cooked meals than restaurant meals. I have two reasons.

First, home-cooked meals are healthier. We can control salt, oil, and ingredients. Second, home-cooked meals save money. Eating out every day is expensive.

For these reasons, home cooking is better. (52 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 50-60 word opinion essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級】Health + Cost の 2 points。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should children have pets at home?

POINTS to consider (use 2): - Responsibility - Allergies - Companionship - Cost

Requirements: 50-60 words.
question_data.sample:
I think children should have pets at home. I have two reasons.

First, pets teach responsibility. Children learn to feed and care for animals daily. Second, pets give companionship. Many children feel less lonely with a pet friend.

For these reasons, pets are good for children. (51 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 50-60 word opinion essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級】Responsibility + Companionship の 2 points。

question_data.passage:
Urbanization and Mental Health

The relationship between urbanization and mental health has become a focus of intensifying research. As more of humanity moves into cities, understanding how urban environments affect psychological well-being has practical importance for public health.

Research consistently shows higher rates of certain mental health conditions in urban populations. Anxiety disorders, depression, and schizophrenia all occur more frequently in urban than rural populations. The differences are substantial and persist after controlling for many demographic factors. Some causes are reasonably well understood: chronic noise, air pollution, social fragmentation, and exposure to traumatic incidents all contribute.

Yet cities also offer mental health resources that rural areas lack. Specialized treatment, peer support communities, and diverse social networks all benefit from urban density. Many people who struggle in small communities find anonymity and acceptance in cities.

The response involves both urban design and mental health systems. Cities designed with green space, walkability, and community gathering places appear to mitigate some negative effects.

questions.stem: What does research show about mental health in urban populations?
choices: Higher rates of anxiety, depression, and schizophrenia in urban populations, persisting after demographic controls
questions.explanation: 第2段落「Anxiety disorders, depression, and schizophrenia all occur more frequently in urban than rural populations ... persist after controlling for many demographic factors」が根拠。

questions.stem: What contributes to higher urban mental health issues?
choices: Chronic noise, air pollution, social fragmentation, exposure to traumatic incidents
questions.explanation: 第2段落「chronic noise, air pollution, social fragmentation, and exposure to traumatic incidents all contribute」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Decline of Coral Reefs

Coral reefs—among Earth's most diverse and beautiful ecosystems—are in dramatic decline. Half of the world's coral has died in recent decades, and the trajectory points toward losses of 70-90% by mid-century even under optimistic warming scenarios.

The primary driver is ocean warming. Corals form symbiotic relationships with algae that provide most of their food and color; warming disrupts this relationship, causing the corals to expel the algae and turn white—'bleaching.' Brief bleaching events can be survived; sustained warming kills the coral.

Additional pressures multiply the damage. Ocean acidification weakens coral skeletons. Pollution from coastal development brings nutrients and sediments that degrade reef systems. Overfishing removes herbivores that keep algae from overgrowing reefs.

The consequences extend beyond coral ecosystems themselves. Reefs support roughly 25% of marine species despite covering less than 1% of the ocean. They protect coastlines from storms—damage that without reefs costs billions annually. They support fisheries on which hundreds of millions of people depend. Tourism revenue from reefs reaches tens of billions of dollars annually.

questions.stem: What is the primary driver of coral decline?
choices: Ocean warming disrupts coral-algae symbiosis, causing bleaching that kills corals when sustained
questions.explanation: 第2段落「The primary driver is ocean warming ... warming disrupts this relationship, causing the corals to expel the algae and turn white—'bleaching.'」が根拠。

questions.stem: What consequences extend beyond coral ecosystems?
choices: Reefs support 25% of marine species, protect coastlines from storms, support fisheries for hundreds of millions, generate tens of billions in tourism
questions.explanation: 第4段落「Reefs support roughly 25% of marine species ... protect coastlines ... support fisheries on which hundreds of millions of people depend ... Tourism revenue ... reaches tens of billions」が根拠。

question_data.passage: What is most striking about contemporary public discourse is not the disagreement among informed people about complex questions, which is to be expected, but the confidence with which uninformed opinions are advanced and the difficulty of correcting them once established.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 現代の言論で最も際立っているのは、複雑な問題について見識ある人々の間で意見が分かれること(それは予想されてしかるべきだ)ではなく、無知な意見が極めて自信を持って表明されること、そして一度定着してしまえばそれを正すことの困難さである。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① What is most striking about X = X について最も際立っているのは ② not A, but B 構造 ③ which is to be expected = それは予想されてしかるべき (関係詞 which 非制限) ④ once established = いったん定着してしまえば。

question_data.passage: It is rarely the most spectacular changes in our circumstances that produce the most lasting transformations of how we live; far more often, it is the gradual accumulation of small adjustments that, almost unnoticed at the time, eventually reshape lives in ways that would have seemed implausible had they been proposed all at once.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 我々の生活のあり方に最も持続的な変容をもたらすのは、状況上の最も劇的な変化であることは稀である。むしろはるかに多くの場合、当時はほとんど気付かれない小さな調整の漸進的な累積こそが、もし一度に提案されたなら信じがたく思われたであろう仕方で、最終的には生活を作り変えるのである。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① It is rarely X that Y = X が Y するのは稀である (強調構文の否定形) ② lasting transformations = 持続的な変容 ③ would have seemed ... had they been proposed = 仮定法過去完了 (倒置 had + S + p.p.) ④ all at once = 一度に。

question_data.passage: To live well requires more than the achievement of one's stated goals, since people often achieve such goals only to discover that what they had wanted was not what they actually needed.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: よく生きるには、自らが掲げた目標を達成すること以上のものが要求される。なぜなら人はそのような目標を達成して初めて、自分が望んでいたものは実際に必要としていたものではなかったと気づくことが多いからである。
questions.explanation: 【京大型】① To live well requires more than X ② achieve ... only to discover = 〜してついに気づくに至る (only to の結果用法) ③ what they had wanted was not what they actually needed = 望んでいたものは実際に必要としていたものではなかった。

question_data.passage: What we call wisdom often consists less in knowing things others do not than in remembering, when it matters, what we already know but tend to forget under pressure.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 我々が「知恵」と呼ぶものは、しばしば、他人が知らないことを知っていることよりもむしろ、肝心な時に、既に知ってはいるが重圧の下では忘れがちなことを思い出すことに存するのである。
questions.explanation: 【京大型】① What we call X = 我々が X と呼ぶもの ② consists less in A than in B = A よりもむしろ B に存する ③ when it matters = 肝心な時に。

question_data.passage: It is precisely when economic conditions favor those who already have advantages that the ideology of personal merit gains greatest currency, since it provides convenient justification for outcomes that might otherwise demand explanation.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 既に優位を持つ者に経済状況が有利に働くまさにその時にこそ、個人の能力主義というイデオロギーは最大の通用力を獲得する。なぜならそれは、そうでなければ説明を要するであろう帰結に対し、好都合な正当化を提供するからである。
questions.explanation: 【一橋型】① It is precisely when ... that ... = まさに〜の時にこそ〜である (強調構文 + 副詞節) ② might otherwise demand explanation = そうでなければ説明を要するであろう (might + otherwise の仮定的文脈)。

question_data.passage: The persistence of irrational beliefs in highly educated populations suggests that what shapes belief is not primarily exposure to evidence but rather the social and emotional contexts in which evidence is encountered.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 高学歴の人々の間で非合理的な信念が持続するという事実は、信念を形作るのが主として証拠への接触ではなく、むしろ証拠に出会う際の社会的・情緒的な文脈であることを示唆している。
questions.explanation: 【一橋型】① The persistence of X ② what shapes belief is not A but rather B ③ the social and emotional contexts in which evidence is encountered = 証拠に出会う際の社会的・情緒的文脈 (関係副詞 in which)。

question_data.passage: The doctor's most difficult task is often not to diagnose disease, which can usually be accomplished with reasonable accuracy through established procedures, but to communicate the diagnosis in ways that respect the patient's autonomy without abandoning them to confusion.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 医師の最も困難な任務はしばしば、確立された手順によって通常は妥当な精度で達成可能な疾患の診断ではなく、患者を混乱に放置することなく、その自律を尊重する形で診断を伝えることである。
questions.explanation: 【国公立医学部型】① not A, but B 構造 ② which can usually be accomplished ... = 通常〜達成可能な (関係詞節) ③ in ways that respect X without abandoning Y = Y を放置することなく X を尊重する仕方で。

question_data.passage: Medicine has advanced more in the last century than in all previous human history combined, and yet the experience of being a patient — being vulnerable, uncertain, dependent on others — remains in essential ways unchanged.

questions.stem: 上記英文を日本語に和訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 医学は過去1世紀で、それ以前のすべての人類史の合計よりも進歩した。それにもかかわらず、患者であるということ、すなわち脆弱で、不確かで、他者に依存するという経験は、本質的な仕方では変わらないままである。
questions.explanation: 【国公立医学部型】① more A than B combined = B の合計よりも A ② and yet = それにもかかわらず ③ in essential ways unchanged = 本質的には変わらない。

questions.stem: 【東大型・自由英作】Some people argue that AI will replace most human jobs within 30 years. Discuss whether this prediction is likely to come true. Write 60-80 words.
questions.answer: I doubt AI will replace most human jobs within 30 years. While AI is transforming routine cognitive tasks, human work involves creativity, emotional intelligence, and complex ethical judgment that current AI cannot replicate. Historical patterns show new technologies typically reshape rather than eliminate work, creating jobs even as they displace others. Substantial transformation is certain; total replacement remains unlikely within this time frame. (62 words)
questions.explanation: 【東大型】立場明示+反論への対応+歴史的論拠+結論。60-80語の自由英作で論理構造 (Position → Reasoning → Concession → Conclusion) を厳守。

questions.stem: 【京大型・自由英作】「失敗から学ぶ方が成功から学ぶよりも価値がある」という意見について、賛成か反対かを述べ、理由を 80-100 語で書きなさい。
questions.answer: I agree that learning from failure is more valuable than learning from success. Failure forces honest analysis: when things go wrong, we must examine our assumptions, decisions, and execution carefully. Success often masks mistakes because outcomes happen to be favorable. Furthermore, failure builds resilience and humility — qualities that prevent future overconfidence. While success teaches what works in particular circumstances, failure teaches transferable lessons about reasoning under uncertainty, which apply broadly across life. (74 words)
questions.explanation: 【京大型・自由英作】Position+3理由 (honest analysis / resilience / transferable lessons) +結論。京大型は「対比」を明確に。

questions.stem: 【阪大型・自由英作】「日本の若者は、もっと積極的に海外で学ぶべきだ」という意見について、賛成か反対かを 100 語程度で書きなさい。
questions.answer: I agree that Japanese young people should study abroad more actively. First, immersive language exposure produces fluency that domestic study cannot match. Second, encountering different cultures fosters the cognitive flexibility increasingly demanded in globalized careers. Third, studying abroad cultivates self-reliance — managing daily life in unfamiliar systems builds problem-solving skills transferable to any future challenge. While financial cost is a legitimate concern, scholarship programs and shorter exchange options have lowered barriers significantly. The investment pays substantial dividends across professional and personal life. (87 words)
questions.explanation: 【阪大型】3理由構造+反論への組込+結論。

questions.stem: 【早稲田型・自由英作】Modern technology has both improved and harmed human relationships. Which effect do you think is greater? Write 100-120 words.
questions.answer: I believe modern technology has improved human relationships overall, despite genuine harms. The benefits are substantial. Video calls let families maintain close bonds across continents. Social media reconnects long-lost friends and supports niche communities of shared interest. Messaging apps enable continuous low-effort connection that was impossible when communication required letters or expensive long-distance calls. The harms are real but narrower: shallow online interactions can displace deeper conversations, and curated online identities create unrealistic comparisons. Yet these costs reflect choices about how we use technology, while the benefits represent genuine expansion of relational possibility. (97 words)
questions.explanation: 【早稲田型】Position → 3 benefits → narrower harms → 'tool vs use' distinction → conclusion。

questions.stem: 【慶應型・自由英作】Some argue universities should focus on practical skills training rather than liberal arts education. Express your view in 120-150 words.
questions.answer: I disagree that universities should prioritize practical skills over liberal arts education. While practical skills are important, three considerations favor maintaining liberal arts. First, specific skills age rapidly: today's cutting-edge tools become obsolete within years, while critical thinking, communication, and historical perspective remain valuable across decades. Second, the most consequential workplace problems — ethical dilemmas, ambiguous strategic choices, communication across diverse stakeholders — require precisely the broad reasoning that liberal arts cultivate. Third, citizens of democracies need the capacity to evaluate competing claims about complex issues; narrow vocational training cannot supply this. The ideal university combines both. Practical skills should supplement, not replace, the broader intellectual formation that has historically distinguished higher education from mere job training. (122 words)
questions.explanation: 【慶應型】Position → 3 reasons → ideal synthesis → conclusion。

question_data.passage:
From: Sarah Lee
To: All Staff
Subject: Office Hours Change

Dear Colleagues,

Starting next Monday (June 5), our office hours will change from 9:00–17:00 to 9:30–17:30. This change reflects feedback from our staff survey indicating preferences for slightly later starts. Lunch break remains 12:00–13:00. For meetings with external clients, the new hours apply automatically; please update your scheduling. If you have specific concerns, please contact the HR team by Friday, June 2.

Best regards,
Sarah Lee

questions.stem: What change will start on June 5?
questions.explanation: Email「office hours will change from 9:00–17:00 to 9:30–17:30」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is the deadline for raising concerns?
questions.explanation: 「contact the HR team by Friday, June 2」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
AUTUMN ART FAIR — Saturday, October 14 
10:00 AM – 5:00 PM | Riverfront Park

Featuring more than 80 local artists. Free entry. Live music from 1:00 PM. Children's workshops (ages 6–12, registration required at the gate). Food trucks on-site. Parking $5; bike racks free. In case of heavy rain, the event moves to City Hall.

Questions? Email info@autumnartfair.org

questions.stem: What activity requires registration?
questions.explanation: 「Children's workshops (ages 6–12, registration required at the gate)」が根拠。

questions.stem: What happens in case of heavy rain?
questions.explanation: 「In case of heavy rain, the event moves to City Hall」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
[Listening Script — to be read aloud at moderate pace]

Woman: Hi Tom, are you free this weekend? Some of us are going hiking on Saturday at Mount Kasuga. Want to come?
Man: Saturday? I'd love to, but my brother is visiting from Osaka that day. Maybe Sunday?
Woman: Sunday works too. We could do an easier trail near the river. Let's meet at 9 AM at the station.
Man: Sounds good. Should I bring lunch?
Woman: Yes, and water. The walk is about 3 hours total.

questions.stem: Why can't Tom go on Saturday?
questions.explanation: スクリプト「my brother is visiting from Osaka that day」が根拠。

questions.stem: What time will they meet on Sunday?
questions.explanation: 「Let's meet at 9 AM at the station」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
[Listening Script Part 1 — short conversation]

W: Excuse me, where can I find the science books?
M: Second floor, near the back. Use the elevator on your right.
W: Thank you. And do you have a Wi-Fi password for visitors?
M: Yes — ask at the help desk on the second floor. They'll write it down for you.

questions.stem: Where are the science books?
questions.explanation: 「Second floor, near the back」が根拠。

questions.stem: Where can the woman get the Wi-Fi password?
choices: At the help desk on second floor
questions.explanation: 「ask at the help desk on the second floor」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
[Listening Script Part 3 — longer conversation]

Professor: Today we're discussing how plants respond to drought. Plants have several defense mechanisms. The most well-known is closing their stomata to reduce water loss. But this comes at a cost — closed stomata reduce CO2 intake, slowing photosynthesis.
Student: How do plants 'decide' when to close stomata?
Professor: They monitor several signals — soil moisture, leaf water content, and a hormone called abscisic acid. When abscisic acid levels rise, that's the signal to close.
Student: Is this an evolved response?
Professor: Yes. Plants in drier regions tend to be more aggressive about closing stomata than plants in wet regions. It's a clear example of local adaptation.

questions.stem: What is the cost of closing stomata?
choices: Reduced CO2 intake and slower photosynthesis
questions.explanation: 「closed stomata reduce CO2 intake, slowing photosynthesis」が根拠。

questions.stem: What hormone signals stomatal closure?
questions.explanation: 「a hormone called abscisic acid ... that's the signal to close」が根拠。

questions.stem: The man ( ) car was stolen reported it to the police.
questions.explanation: 先行詞 the man (人) + 所有関係 → whose。

questions.stem: If I ( ) more time, I would visit Kyoto.
questions.explanation: 仮定法過去 If S 過去形 → 主節 would + 動詞原形。

questions.stem: It was the rain ( ) caused the cancellation.
questions.explanation: It is/was X that ... = 強調構文。

question_data.passage:
Most discussions of education reform focus on changes to curriculum, technology, or assessment. Yet research suggests that a far more consequential factor is teacher quality — the actual pedagogical skill, subject knowledge, and engagement of the people in classrooms. Replacing curriculum without changing teachers produces small effects; investing in teacher training, mentorship, and retention produces large effects.

This pattern has uncomfortable implications. Reforms that focus on visible structural changes — new technology, new textbooks, new tests — are easier to implement than reforms that change who teaches and how well they are supported.

School systems that produce strong outcomes share a common feature: they treat teaching as a serious profession requiring substantial preparation, ongoing development, and adequate compensation.

questions.stem: 【東大型・要約 60-80字】上記英文の主旨を 60-80 字の日本語で要約せよ。
questions.answer: 教育改革で最も重要なのはカリキュラムや技術ではなく、教師の質である。構造改革は実施しやすいが効果は小さく、教員研修・指導・定着への投資の方が大きな成果を生む。(78字)
questions.explanation: 【東大型・要約】① teacher quality > 構造改革 ② 構造改革は visible だが効果小 ③ 教職への適切な処遇が outcome を決める。

question_data.passage:
In the design of buildings, infrastructure, and other long-lived systems, decisions made today commit future generations to consequences they cannot easily change. A bridge built today shapes traffic flows for fifty years. A power plant operates for decades, locking in a particular energy mix. A city's basic layout influences daily life for centuries.

The choices involve genuine trade-offs that cannot be resolved by purely technical analysis. Cheaper options often have higher long-term costs. More resilient designs require larger upfront investment.

Democratic processes for making these choices have proved difficult to design. The decisions require technical knowledge that most citizens lack. The time horizons exceed the careers of most decision-makers. The communities affected often include people not yet born.

The broader implication is that the design of physical infrastructure is also the design of social futures. Communities that take this seriously may produce environments that serve human flourishing better than communities that treat infrastructure as merely technical.

questions.stem: 下線部 'the design of physical infrastructure is also the design of social futures' を日本語に訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 物理的なインフラの設計は、社会の将来の設計でもあるのである。
questions.explanation: 【東工大型】① the design of X = X の設計 ② physical infrastructure = 物理的インフラ ③ is also the design of social futures = 社会の将来の設計でもある。

question_data.passage:
In recent years, economic researchers have given increasing attention to a phenomenon they call 'job polarization.' In many wealthy countries, employment growth has been concentrated at the top and bottom of the wage distribution while middle-wage employment has stagnated or declined.

The technological mechanism is reasonably well understood. Routine middle-skill tasks — bookkeeping, manufacturing assembly, certain analytical work — have been increasingly automated, while high-skill tasks requiring complex cognition and low-skill tasks requiring physical dexterity in unstructured environments have proved harder to automate. The result is hollowing-out of the middle of the labor market.

questions.stem: What is 'job polarization' according to the passage?
choices: Employment growth at the top and bottom of wage distribution while middle-wage employment stagnates or declines
questions.explanation: 第1段落「employment growth has been concentrated at the top and bottom of the wage distribution while middle-wage employment has stagnated or declined」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The historical record of pandemics is filled with patterns that recur with troubling regularity. Initial denial and minimization. Belated emergency responses. Rapid spread through population centers. Disproportionate effects on the disadvantaged. Eventual containment through some combination of intervention, behavioral change, and natural immunity. And then, distressingly often, partial forgetting that leaves societies poorly prepared for the next pandemic.

The COVID-19 pandemic followed this familiar pattern in many ways. Initial responses were slow and inconsistent. Yet some elements differed substantially. mRNA vaccines were developed and tested in months rather than years.

questions.stem: What recurring pandemic pattern does the author identify?
choices: Denial → belated response → rapid spread → disproportionate harm → eventual containment → partial forgetting
questions.explanation: 第1段落の patterns 列挙が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Among the more striking features of contemporary cultural life is the simultaneous expansion and fragmentation of available content. Never before have so many books, films, music recordings, and other cultural products been accessible to so many people. Yet the audiences for any particular work, with rare exceptions, have shrunk.

This pattern reflects multiple drivers. Digital distribution has dramatically reduced the costs of making content available. Algorithm-driven discovery has produced content recommendations tailored to narrow tastes. Traditional gatekeepers — publishers, broadcasters, critics — have lost much of their influence.

questions.stem: What pattern does the author identify in contemporary culture?
choices: Simultaneous expansion (more content available) and fragmentation (audiences for any particular work shrunk)
questions.explanation: 第1段落「the simultaneous expansion and fragmentation of available content」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
There is a feature of contemporary discourse about happiness and well-being that deserves more critical examination than it usually receives. The 'positivity industry' — the books, courses, and apps promoting strategies for personal happiness — typically locates the sources of well-being in individual psychology and individual practices.

Research on happiness and well-being suggests a more complex picture. While individual factors do matter, structural and relational factors often matter more. Stable relationships, meaningful work, financial security, and supportive communities affect well-being substantially.

The individualist framing of happiness has consequences. It implicitly absolves social arrangements from responsibility for the well-being they produce or fail to produce.

questions.stem: What critique does the author offer of the 'positivity industry'?
choices: It locates well-being sources in individual psychology, ignoring structural/relational factors that often matter more, and absolves social arrangements from responsibility
questions.explanation: 第1-3段落「typically locates the sources of well-being in individual psychology ... structural and relational factors often matter more ... absolves social arrangements from responsibility」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Throughout the twentieth century, scientific discoveries about the human brain transformed our understanding of mental life. The brain was revealed not as a unified thinking machine but as a collection of specialized regions, networks, and processes that interact in complex ways to produce experience and behavior.

These discoveries have practical implications for fields ranging from education to law. Educational practices that ignored the limited capacity of working memory often produced poor results despite abundant effort. Legal systems that assumed defendants had complete control over their behavior have struggled to handle cases involving mental illness, addiction, or impulsive decisions.

questions.stem: What practical implications does brain research have?
choices: Reshapes education (working memory limits) and law (limits of conscious control over behavior)
questions.explanation: 第2段落「Educational practices that ignored the limited capacity of working memory ... Legal systems that assumed defendants had complete control over their behavior have struggled」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The phenomenon of forgetting has long puzzled philosophers and psychologists. We forget vast amounts of what we experience: most of our daily activities, most of our conversations, most of what we read.

Research suggests that forgetting is not a failure of memory but rather an essential feature of effective cognition. Storage capacity may be effectively unlimited in principle, but retrieval becomes increasingly difficult as more potential matches accumulate. A perfect memory of every experience would make finding relevant information nearly impossible amid the flood of irrelevant retrievals.

What we retain reflects implicit predictions about what may matter later. Emotional events tend to be remembered more vividly because their emotional content suggests potential relevance.

questions.stem: Why does the brain forget according to the passage?
choices: Forgetting is an essential cognitive feature: pruning unlikely-to-be-needed information keeps memory functional and retrieval feasible
questions.explanation: 第2段落「forgetting is not a failure of memory but rather an essential feature of effective cognition」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Among the most studied features of contemporary economies is the rising importance of intangible assets. Where earlier generations of capital — factories, machinery, inventory — was largely physical, modern economies depend increasingly on assets that cannot be touched: software, brands, organizational practices, accumulated data, network relationships.

Intangible assets have unusual properties. They scale at near-zero marginal cost — software written once can serve many users without proportional resource expansion. They benefit from network effects in which value rises with usage. They depend heavily on continuous investment in maintenance and updating.

questions.stem: What unusual properties do intangible assets have according to the passage?
choices: Scale at near-zero marginal cost, network effects, rapid depreciation without continuous investment
questions.explanation: 第2段落「scale at near-zero marginal cost ... network effects ... depreciate rapidly without sustained attention」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In the long span of human history, the period during which population grew rapidly is brief and atypical. For most of our species' existence, populations grew slowly when they grew at all. Only with industrialization, public health improvements, and agricultural transformation did sustained rapid growth become the norm in much of the world.

The demographic transition — the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates — has now occurred in nearly every country, though at different times and rates. The result is that global population growth is decelerating, with the world likely to reach peak population somewhere between 2080 and 2100.

questions.stem: What does the demographic transition refer to?
choices: Shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates, now occurred in nearly every country
questions.explanation: 第2段落「the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates — has now occurred in nearly every country」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In recent years, scholars have increasingly studied how cognitive and emotional biases affect decisions in domains where careful reasoning would seem to be most expected. Doctors making diagnostic decisions, judges making sentencing decisions, scientists evaluating research, and investors making financial decisions all show systematic patterns of bias even when the stakes are high.

The biases follow recognizable patterns. Anchoring effects make initial information disproportionately influential. Confirmation bias leads to greater weight given to evidence supporting current views. Representativeness heuristics produce judgments based on superficial similarities to typical cases. Availability biases give weight to easily recalled examples regardless of their representativeness.

The practical implications are significant. Domains that depend on accurate reasoning have begun developing techniques to mitigate bias effects. Structured decision protocols force consideration of factors that intuition might overlook. Diverse perspectives can identify biases that individual decision-makers cannot see in themselves.

questions.stem: What practical mitigation techniques does the author identify?
choices: Structured decision protocols, diverse perspectives, awareness training, algorithmic decision support used carefully
questions.explanation: 第3段落「Structured decision protocols ... Diverse perspectives ... Training programs ... Algorithmic decision support」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Among the most striking developments in recent decades has been the rise of professional services as a major economic sector. Where earlier industrial economies were dominated by manufacturing, modern wealthy economies increasingly depend on services delivered by professionally trained workers — accounting, legal services, healthcare, education, consulting, software development, design.

The shift has consequences for inequality, geographic concentration, and the structure of opportunity. Professional services tend to concentrate in metropolitan regions, contributing to geographic inequality. Returns to professional credentials have risen, creating barriers for those without access to higher education.

questions.stem: What consequences of professional services rise does the author identify?
choices: Geographic concentration in metros, rising returns to credentials, winner-take-most dynamics
questions.explanation: 第3段落「concentrate in metropolitan regions, contributing to geographic inequality ... Returns to professional credentials have risen」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In any examination of the relationship between technology and society, a recurring question concerns whether technological change drives social change or social arrangements drive which technologies develop and how they are deployed. The most informed answers reject the dichotomy. Technology and society are mutually shaping; particular technological possibilities are shaped by social demands and resources, while social arrangements are reshaped by available technologies in turn.

Historical examples illustrate this mutual shaping. The printing press enabled certain forms of mass communication, but the social and political contexts in which it operated determined whether it spread literacy or fueled religious conflict.

questions.stem: What does the author argue about the technology-society relationship?
choices: Mutually shaping: tech possibilities shaped by social demands; social arrangements reshaped by available tech in turn
questions.explanation: 第1段落「Technology and society are mutually shaping」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
Among the more puzzling features of contemporary academic life is the gap between what scholars produce and what reaches broader publics. Substantial research is conducted, books are written, articles published — yet much of this work remains read primarily by other specialists in narrow fields.

Multiple factors contribute. Academic incentive structures reward publications in specialized journals more than works aimed at broader audiences. The vocabularies and conventions of academic writing often discourage non-specialist readers. Career paths designed for specialized research do not develop the writing and communication skills required for broader engagement.

questions.stem: What contributes to the gap between academic work and broader publics?
choices: Academic incentives reward specialized publications, academic vocabularies discourage non-specialists, career paths don't develop broader communication skills
questions.explanation: 第2段落「Academic incentive structures reward publications in specialized journals ... vocabularies and conventions ... discourage non-specialist readers」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In recent decades, scholars have increasingly studied a phenomenon sometimes called 'attention residue' — the diminished cognitive capacity that remains after switching from one task to another. Even when we mentally commit to a new task, traces of the previous task continue to occupy cognitive resources, reducing our effectiveness on what we are now doing.

Research suggests this residue persists for substantial periods after task switches. Returning attention to deep work after even brief interruptions takes much longer than the interruption itself. The cumulative effect of frequent switching is significant degradation of cognitive performance.

questions.stem: What is 'attention residue'?
choices: Diminished cognitive capacity that remains after task switches; traces of previous task continue to occupy cognitive resources
questions.explanation: 第1段落「'attention residue' — the diminished cognitive capacity that remains after switching from one task to another」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In considerations of artificial intelligence, attention has often focused on dramatic possibilities — superhuman general intelligence, autonomous weapons, mass unemployment. Yet many of the most consequential effects are likely to be more subtle: gradual shifts in how decisions get made, how information is filtered, how human capabilities develop.

Decision support systems already influence many domains. Hiring algorithms shape who gets job interviews. Recommendation systems shape what content people consume. Medical decision aids shape diagnostic and treatment choices. Risk assessment tools shape sentencing and parole decisions.

The gradual shift of decision-making toward algorithmic systems has implications that go beyond accuracy questions. Human judgment that goes unused atrophies; the capacity to make decisions that algorithms now make may decline if the algorithms become unavailable.

questions.stem: What subtle but consequential AI effects does the author identify?
choices: Gradual shifts in decision-making, information filtering, human capability development; encoding biases; atrophy of unused judgment
questions.explanation: 第1-3段落「subtle: gradual shifts in how decisions get made, how information is filtered, how human capabilities develop ... unused atrophies」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
In examinations of contemporary economic life, a recurring observation concerns the rising importance of platforms — businesses whose primary function is connecting other parties for transactions or interactions. Ride-hailing platforms connect drivers and passengers. App stores connect software developers and users. Online marketplaces connect merchants and shoppers.

Platforms operate by different logic than traditional businesses. They benefit from network effects. They tend toward winner-take-most outcomes, since users gravitate toward the most populated platforms. They derive much of their value from controlling and analyzing data about transactions they intermediate.

The rise of platforms has substantial consequences. The conditions of work for those whose income depends on platforms — drivers, deliverers, content creators — often differ substantially from traditional employment, with reduced security and bargaining power.

questions.stem: What consequences of platform rise does the author identify?
choices: Reduced worker security/bargaining power, network effects driving winner-take-most outcomes
questions.explanation: 第3段落「reduced security and bargaining power」+ 第2段落「winner-take-most outcomes」が根拠。

questions.stem: ヒトの呼吸において、肺胞でのガス交換に関わる主な気体はどれか。
questions.explanation: 肺胞のガス交換は外気から O2 を血液へ取り込み、血液中の CO2 を外気へ排出する。

questions.stem: 光合成と呼吸について正しい記述はどれか。
choices: どちらも生体内の代謝経路である
光合成は分解、呼吸は合成
questions.explanation: 光合成は CO2+H2O → 有機物+O2 (合成・同化)、呼吸は有機物+O2 → CO2+H2O+ATP (分解・異化)。両方とも生体代謝。

questions.stem: DNA の塩基対形成において、A (アデニン) と対になるのはどれか。
questions.explanation: DNA の塩基対は A-T (2本水素結合)、G-C (3本水素結合)。RNA では T が U に置換され A-U。

questions.stem: 次のうちタンパク質の構成単位はどれか。
questions.explanation: タンパク質はアミノ酸 (20種類) のペプチド結合の重合体。

questions.stem: 【MARCH 標準】塩化ナトリウム NaCl 5.85 g を水に溶かして全量 250 mL とした水溶液のモル濃度を求めよ。原子量 Na = 23, Cl = 35.5。
水素イオン濃度 [H+] = 1.0 mol/L の水溶液の pH を求めよ。
【MARCH 標準】メタン CH4 を完全燃焼させたときの化学反応式を書け。
questions.explanation: 炭素 1 個 → CO2 1 個。水素 4 個 → H2O 2 個。酸素は左辺で 4 個必要 → O2 2 個。

questions.stem: H2 1 mol が完全燃焼して水ができる時の生成水の物質量と質量を求めよ。原子量 H=1, O=16。
questions.explanation: 2H2 + O2 → 2H2O。1 mol H2 → 1 mol H2O。M(H2O) = 18 → 18 g。

questions.stem: 【東大型】平衡定数 K = 4.0 の反応 A + B ⇌ 2C で、初期に [A]=[B]=1.0 mol/L、[C]=0 だった。平衡時の [C] の近似値を求めよ。
【東大型】芳香族化合物のニトロ化、スルホン化、ハロゲン化はいずれも何反応に分類されるか。
questions.answer: 芳香族求電子置換反応 (Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution)
questions.explanation: 【東大型】芳香族環の H が求電子試薬 (NO2+, SO3, X+) に置換される反応群。

questions.stem: 【東大型】電池反応 Zn + Cu^2+ → Zn^2+ + Cu のうち、酸化される元素はどれか。電子数の移動も示せ。
questions.answer: Zn が 2 個の電子を放出して酸化される (Zn → Zn^2+ + 2e-)。Cu^2+ が電子を受け取って還元される。
questions.explanation: 【東大型・酸化還元】酸化数変化: Zn (0) → Zn^2+ (+2) で酸化、Cu^2+ (+2) → Cu (0) で還元。

questions.stem: 【東大型】一次反応 A → 生成物 で A の半減期 t_(1/2) と速度定数 k の関係を導け。
questions.explanation: 【東大型・一次反応】[A] = [A]0 exp(-kt)。半減期で [A] = [A]0/2 → exp(-kt_(1/2)) = 1/2 → t_(1/2) = ln 2/k。一次反応の半減期は初期濃度に依らない。

questions.stem: sin 30° + cos 60° + tan 45° の値を求めよ。
2log10(2) + log10(25) を簡単にせよ。
等比数列の初項 2, 公比 3 で第5項までの和 S_5 を求めよ。
f(x) = 2x^2 - 4x + 3 の x = 1 における接線の方程式を求めよ。
【名大型】3つのサイコロを同時に振って出た目の和が 10 になる確率を求めよ。
questions.explanation: 【名大型・確率】3つのサイコロの目の組合せで和 10 になる順序付き組合せは 27 通り。全 216 通り。確率 = 27/216 = 1/8。

questions.stem: 【名大型】曲線 y = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x の極値と変曲点を求めよ。
【早稲田理工型】f(x) = x e^{-x} の最大値 (x ≥ 0) を求めよ。
【早稲田理工型】定積分 ∫_0^π sin^2 x dx を求めよ。
【上智理工型】複素数 (1+i)^4 を展開して a+bi 形式で答えよ。
【上智理工型】関数 y = log(x^2 + 1) の導関数 y' を求めよ (自然対数)。
【国公立医学部型】sin θ + cos θ = 1/2 のとき sin θ · cos θ の値を求めよ。
questions.explanation: (sin θ + cos θ)^2 = 1/4。sin^2 θ + cos^2 θ + 2sinθcosθ = 1/4 → 1 + 2sinθcosθ = 1/4 → sinθcosθ = -3/8。

questions.stem: 【国公立医学部型】正規分布 N(0, 1) で確率 P(0 ≤ Z ≤ 1) ≈ 0.3413 とすると P(-1 ≤ Z ≤ 1) を求めよ。
【私立医学部型】二次関数 y = x^2 + bx + c が x = 1 で最小値 -3 をとる。b, c の値を求めよ。
【私立医学部型】行列 A = ((2,1),(1,2)) の固有値を求めよ。
【東大型】曲線 y = sin x (0 ≤ x ≤ π) と x 軸で囲まれる領域の面積を求めよ。
【東大型】関数 f(x) = (sin x)/x の x → 0 における極限を求め、l'Hôpital を使わず証明せよ。
questions.explanation: 単位円で 0 < x < π/2 のとき面積比較から sin x < x < tan x → 1 < x/sin x < 1/cos x → cos x < sinx/x < 1。x→0 ではさみうちで → 1。

questions.stem: 【京大型】2つの実数 x, y が x^2 + y^2 = 1 を満たすとき、x + y の最大値・最小値を求めよ。
questions.explanation: x = cos θ, y = sin θ とおく。x + y = √2 sin(θ + π/4)。最大値 √2、最小値 -√2。

questions.stem: 【京大型】放物線 y^2 = 4x の焦点と準線を求めよ。
【慶應理工型】|\vec{a}|=2, |\vec{b}|=3, \vec{a}·\vec{b}=4 のとき |\vec{a}+\vec{b}| を求めよ。
【慶應理工型】数列 1, 2, 4, 8, ... の第10項と初項から第10項までの和を求めよ。
【東大型】方程式 x^4 + 4x^3 + 6x^2 + 4x + 1 = 0 の解を求めよ。
【東大型】f が連続で f(0)=−1, f(1)=2 ならば、f(x)=0 となる x が (0,1) に存在することを中間値の定理で示せ。
questions.answer: f は [0,1] で連続、f(0)=-1<0<2=f(1) より中間値の定理から f(c)=0 となる c ∈ (0,1) が存在する。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】定理の前提 (連続性 + 端点の符号反転) を明示。

questions.stem: 質量 m = 0.50 kg の物体が水平面上で加速度 a = 2.0 m/s^2 で運動している。物体に作用する力 F を求めよ (摩擦無視)。
questions.explanation: 運動方程式 F = ma。

questions.stem: 長さ 0.50 m の単振り子の周期 T を求めよ。g = 9.8 m/s^2、π ≈ 3.14。
電圧 12 V の電池に抵抗 4 Ω を接続したときの電流 I を求めよ。
questions.explanation: オームの法則 V = IR → I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A。

questions.stem: 熱容量 C = 100 J/K の物質の温度を 20℃ から 80℃ まで上げるのに必要な熱量 Q を求めよ。
【東工大型】質量 m の物体が地面から鉛直上方に初速 v_0 で投げ上げられた。最高点に到達するまでの時間 t と最高点の高さ h を求めよ。空気抵抗無視、g = 重力加速度。
【東工大型】点電荷 q1 = 1 μC, q2 = 2 μC が距離 r = 1 m 離れている。両電荷間に働くクーロン力 F の大きさを求めよ。k = 9.0×10^9 N·m^2/C^2。
【早稲田理工型】波長 λ, 振動数 f の波の伝播速度 v を表せ。
questions.explanation: 波の基本式。

questions.stem: 【早稲田理工型】質量 m, 速度 v の物体の運動量 p を表せ。
questions.explanation: 運動量定義式。ベクトル量。SI 単位 kg·m/s。

questions.stem: 【私立医学部型】質量 m, 速度 v の物体が壁に弾性衝突して速度 -v で跳ね返った。物体が壁に与えた力積を求めよ。
questions.answer: 壁が受けた力積は 2mv (物体から壁へ)
questions.explanation: 物体の運動量変化 = m(-v) - mv = -2mv → 物体が壁に与える力積は反作用 = +2mv。

questions.stem: 【私立医学部型】波長 10^-10 m の X 線のエネルギー (J) を求めよ。プランク定数 h = 6.6×10^-34 J·s, c = 3.0×10^8 m/s。
【東大型】容器内の理想気体 (単原子分子) の内部エネルギー U を温度 T で表せ。気体定数 R, モル数 n。
questions.explanation: 単原子分子の平均運動エネルギー (3/2)kT/分子 → n mol で (3/2)nRT。等容モル比熱 C_v = (3/2)R。

questions.stem: 【東大型】RC 回路で電源 V, 抵抗 R, コンデンサ C のとき、t=0 でスイッチを閉じてからコンデンサにかかる電圧 v(t) を求めよ。
questions.explanation: KVL: V = iR + v_C, i = C dv_C/dt → V = RC dv_C/dt + v_C → v_C(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})。時定数 τ = RC。
